step_by_step_thinking:
The patient experienced shock shortly after delivering normally. Among the listed possibilities—Amniotic Fluid Embolism (AFE), Post Partum Hemorrhage (PPH), Uterine Inversion, and Eclampsia—the evidence suggests that **\boxed{A}** seems least consistent because it presents abruptly and outside typical scenarios where it might manifest, whereas eclampsia aligns well with known symptoms like high blood pressure and seizures occurring around the time of delivery.

Clinical Reasoning:
Given the abrupt nature of shock immediately following delivery, while all conditions can potentially cause shock, Amniotic Fluid Embolism (AFE) specifically tends to occur unpredictably during labor or delivery and doesn't fit the pattern seen in the patient's scenario. On the contrary, Eclampsia fits the profile well with possible underlying hypertension leading to maternal-seizure followed by shock upon resolution of the episode. Therefore,
